title: Multiple Loans Risk
fullRewardHint: Answer all {{total}} choices correctly to earn the full reward.
reflectionPrompts: How can you assess whether you're taking on too much debt across multiple loans?
What warning signs indicate you're approaching your borrowing capacity limits?

stages.prompt: Taking many loans at once leads to:

options.label: Faster growth and financial advancement
options.reflection: While multiple loans might seem to accelerate goals, they actually create overwhelming repayment obligations that can derail financial progress.

options.label: More opportunities to invest and build wealth
options.reflection: Multiple loans increase debt obligations rather than investment capacity. True wealth building requires managing existing debt first.

options.label: Greater financial flexibility and options
options.reflection: Multiple loan payments actually reduce flexibility by committing large portions of your income to debt service rather than choices.

options.label: Higher repayment stress and financial burden
options.reflection: Exactly! Multiple loans multiply your monthly obligations, creating stress and increasing the risk of default.

stages.prompt: What's the main danger of loan stacking?

options.label: Missing out on loan benefits and rewards
options.reflection: The real danger isn't missed rewards - it's the overwhelming debt burden that makes meeting basic financial obligations difficult.

options.label: Increased risk of default and credit damage
options.reflection: Correct! Multiple loans increase the likelihood of missed payments, defaults, and serious damage to your credit score.

options.label: Difficulty getting future loan approvals
options.reflection: While future approvals may be affected, the immediate danger is managing current obligations and avoiding default on existing loans.

options.label: Harder to track all payment due dates
options.reflection: Organization challenges are secondary concerns. The primary risk is the financial strain that makes timely payments nearly impossible.

stages.prompt: How does multiple loan debt affect your financial health?

options.label: Improves your debt-to-income ratio
options.reflection: Multiple loans actually worsen your debt-to-income ratio by increasing total debt obligations relative to your income.

options.label: Has no significant impact on financial metrics
options.reflection: Multiple loans significantly impact key financial ratios and create measurable stress on your ability to meet all financial obligations.

options.label: Worsens your debt-to-income ratio and financial stability
options.reflection: Perfect! More loans mean higher debt obligations relative to income, reducing financial stability and borrowing capacity.

options.label: Temporarily affects credit until loans are paid off
options.reflection: The impact isn't temporary - multiple active loans continuously strain your finances and credit profile while outstanding.

stages.prompt: What should you prioritize when considering multiple loans?

options.label: Ensuring you can manage all payments comfortably
options.reflection: Exactly! Financial health requires confirming you can handle total debt obligations without stress before taking on additional loans.

options.label: Getting funds quickly for immediate needs
options.reflection: Speed shouldn't override financial responsibility. Prioritize your ability to manage existing obligations before taking on new debt.

options.label: Finding the lowest interest rates available
options.reflection: While interest rates matter, they're secondary to whether you can actually afford the combined payments from all your loans.

options.label: Choosing lenders with convenient payment options
options.reflection: Convenience features don't address the fundamental challenge of managing multiple debt obligations simultaneously.

stages.prompt: What's the golden rule for responsible borrowing with multiple needs?

options.label: Consolidate needs into one larger loan
options.reflection: Consolidation can help, but the fundamental principle is limiting total debt to what you can comfortably repay, regardless of structure.

options.label: Only borrow what fits your repayment capacity
options.reflection: Perfect! This rule prevents over-leveraging and ensures you maintain financial stability while meeting all debt obligations.

options.label: Spread loans over time to reduce monthly impact
options.reflection: Timing strategies don't eliminate the core issue - if total debt exceeds your repayment capacity, problems will eventually surface.

options.label: Keep some loans for emergency backup funding
options.reflection: Emergency funds should come from savings, not additional debt. Backup borrowing creates more financial risk, not security.
